1.synthesis: God speaks the Ten Words, prefacing them with grace: “I am the LORD your God, who brought you out of the land of Egypt, out of the house of slavery” (v.2). The first table governs love for God — no other gods, no images, no misuse of his name, the Sabbath (vv.3–11); the second governs love for neighbor — honor parents, and no murder, adultery, theft, false witness, or coveting (vv.12–17). The integrative thread is the moral heart of the covenant: redemption issues in a way of life that reflects God’s own character, summed up as love for God and neighbor, the abiding standard of righteousness given by the Redeemer to the redeemed.
1.historical_context: The Decalogue, “the ten words” (Exodus 34:28), is the covenant’s constitution, written by God’s own finger on stone (Exodus 31:18) and placed in the ark. The prologue grounding law in deliverance (v.2) shows the commandments are not a ladder to earn favor but the grateful shape of life for those already redeemed. The Sabbath command is grounded here in creation (v.11) and in Deuteronomy 5:15 in redemption. Traditions number the commandments slightly differently, but the substance is one.
1.literary_structure: The two tables (vv.3–11, vv.12–17) correspond to the two great commandments into which Jesus gathers the whole law (Matthew 22:37–40). The movement from God to neighbor, and the climactic inward command against coveting (v.17), show that the law reaches the heart, not merely outward acts. The grace-prologue (v.2) frames all that follows.
1.theological_application: The moral law remains God’s enduring standard of righteousness, exposing sin (Romans 7:7), restraining evil, and guiding the redeemed in grateful obedience. It cannot justify — “by works of the law no human being will be justified” (Romans 3:20) — yet it reveals God’s holy character and our need of a Savior. Believers, freed from the law as a covenant of works, delight in it as the rule of love (Romans 13:8–10; Psalm 119:97). Rightly understood, the law is a gift, revealing both the character of God and the contours of a flourishing human life (Psalm 19:7–11). It humbles us by exposing how far we fall short, driving us to the Savior, and then, received in Christ, it guides the grateful heart in the path of love. The believer neither despises the commandments as bondage nor trusts them for salvation, but delights in them as the wisdom of a redeeming God (Romans 7:12).
1.christology: Christ did not abolish the law but fulfilled it (Matthew 5:17), perfectly keeping its every command and bearing its curse for transgressors (Galatians 3:13). He is the end of the law for righteousness to everyone who believes (Romans 10:4), and by the Spirit he writes the law on the hearts of his people so that its righteous requirement is fulfilled in them (Romans 8:4; Jeremiah 31:33). The law given by the Redeemer at Sinai is honored, satisfied, and internalized in the greater Redeemer.

18.synthesis: Witnessing the thunder, lightning, trumpet, and smoking mountain, the people tremble and stand far off, begging Moses, “You speak to us, and we will listen; but do not let God speak to us, lest we die” (v.19). Moses answers, “Do not fear, for God has come to test you, that the fear of him may be before you, that you may not sin” (v.20). “The people stood far off, while Moses drew near to the thick darkness where God was” (v.21). The integrative thread is the necessity and limit of mediation: confronted with God’s holiness, the people cannot bear his direct voice and plead for a mediator — and Moses, drawing near the darkness, both meets the need and points beyond himself.
18.historical_context: The people’s terror (vv.18–19) and their request for a mediator are recalled approvingly in Deuteronomy 5:23–28, where God grants the request and promises a prophet like Moses. Moses’ distinction between slavish dread and the godly “fear of him” that restrains sin (v.20) is a key biblical nuance. “The thick darkness where God was” (v.21) expresses the hidden, unapproachable majesty of God (1 Kings 8:12).
18.literary_structure: This response to the Decalogue (vv.18–21) interprets the people’s relationship to the law: they need someone to stand between. The contrast between the people standing “far off” and Moses drawing near to the darkness (v.21) is the visual heart of the passage and the seed of the prophet-like-Moses promise that runs to Christ (Deuteronomy 18:15–18).
18.theological_application: There is a fear that drives from God and a fear that draws to holiness; Moses distinguishes them (v.20). The right fear of God is a gift that guards against sin (Proverbs 16:6), not a cringing terror that fellowship with God through a mediator dispels. The passage shows that fallen people instinctively know they need a go-between, and prepares for the gospel answer to that need (1 Timothy 2:5). The instinctive cry for a mediator (v.19) reveals a truth written on the conscience: sinful people cannot stand before a holy God unaided. Moses’ careful distinction between paralyzing dread and the sanctifying fear that keeps us from sin (v.20) teaches that true reverence does not flee from God but is drawn, through a mediator, into holy nearness — the very nearness the gospel secures (Hebrews 10:22).
18.christology: The people’s cry for a mediator (v.19) is answered ultimately in the prophet like Moses, Jesus Christ (Acts 3:22; Deuteronomy 18:18), who alone draws near to God on our behalf and brings God’s word to us. Where Moses entered the thick darkness and the people stood afar, Christ enters the true holy place and brings his people near (Hebrews 9:24). The mediator who calms the people’s terror prefigures the one who says, “Do not fear,” and grants confident access to the Father (Ephesians 2:18).

22.synthesis: The first of the covenant laws concerns worship: “You shall not make gods of silver to be with me… An altar of earth you shall make for me” (vv.23–24). Sacrifices are to be offered where God “causes my name to be remembered,” and he will come and bless (v.24); altars of stone must be uncut, “for if you wield your tool on it you profane it,” and no steps may expose nakedness (vv.25–26). The integrative thread is the regulation of approach to the holy God: having forbidden idols, God specifies how he is to be worshiped, on his terms, in simplicity and reverence — worship is not invented by man but ordered by God.
22.historical_context: These altar laws (vv.24–26) open the “Book of the Covenant” (Exodus 24:7), the case-laws of chapters 21–23. The earth or uncut-stone altar (vv.24–25) contrasts with the elaborate, human-glorifying shrines of the nations; unhewn stone keeps worship from becoming a display of human craft. The prohibition of steps and exposure (v.26) guards the dignity and modesty of approach, later addressed by the priests’ garments (Exodus 28:42).
22.literary_structure: Placed immediately after the people’s plea for a mediator (Exodus 20:21), the altar laws begin the detailed covenant code with the matter of worship, signaling its priority. The promise “I will come to you and bless you” (v.24) ties acceptable worship to God’s gracious presence, anticipating the tabernacle where his name will dwell.
22.theological_application: God regulates his own worship; we may not approach him by our own invention but as he prescribes (Leviticus 10:1–3; John 4:24). The simplicity of the earthen altar rebukes worship that glorifies human artistry over God, and the promise of blessing where God records his name (v.24) assures that he meets his people in the worship he appoints. Reverence and obedience, not novelty, mark true worship. The principle that God prescribes his own worship guards the church in every age against the endless human temptation to refashion God after our own imagination (Deuteronomy 12:32). The earthen simplicity of the altar rebukes worship that exalts human achievement, and the promise “I will come to you and bless you” (v.24) assures that God graciously meets his people precisely in the humble, obedient worship he himself appoints.
22.christology: The altar where God causes his name to dwell and comes to bless (v.24) finds its fulfillment in Christ, in whom God’s name and presence dwell bodily (Colossians 2:9) and through whom alone we have an altar and acceptable worship (Hebrews 13:10; 13:15). The unhewn altar, untouched by human tools, hints that our approach to God rests not on works of our own making but on what God provides — fulfilled in the once-for-all sacrifice of Christ (Ephesians 2:8–9).
